questions.question:
Data forwarding techniques can be used to speed up operation in the presence of data dependencies. Consider the following replacements of LHS with RHS:

i.   LHS: R1 <- Loc, R2 <- Loc        RHS: R1 <- Loc, R2 <- R1
ii.  LHS: R1 <- Loc, R2 <- Loc        RHS: R2 <- Loc, R1 <- Loc
iii. LHS: R1 <- Loc, R2 <- Loc        RHS: R2 <- Loc, R1 <- R2
iv.  LHS: R1 <- R2 + R3, R2 <- R1 + R3  RHS: R1 <- R2 + R3, R2 <- R2 + R3 + R3

In which of the following options, will the result of executing the RHS be the same as executing the LHS irrespective of the instructions preceding and following them?

A. i and ii
B. i and iv
C. ii and iii
D. ii and iv
questions.to_find: Which replacement pairs (LHS -> RHS) produce identical results regardless of surrounding instructions

understand.plain: Data forwarding allows a pipeline to use a computed result before it is written back to memory or a register, avoiding stalls. Here we check if replacing an instruction sequence (LHS) with another (RHS) always produces the same final register values, no matter what instructions come before or after. If the two sequences are semantically equivalent in isolation AND also do not change how adjacent instructions interact, the replacement is safe.

keywords.explain: A hardware technique that routes a computed value directly to where it is needed in the pipeline without waiting for the write-back stage.
keywords.example: If R1 is computed in stage 3 and needed in stage 4, forwarding avoids a stall by passing the value directly.

keywords.explain: A situation where an instruction requires the result of a previous instruction that has not yet completed.
keywords.example: ADD R2, R1, R3 followed by ADD R4, R2, R5 -- the second instruction depends on R2 from the first.

keywords.explain: The most common data hazard: an instruction tries to read a register that a prior instruction has not yet written.
keywords.example: R1 <- A; R2 <- R1 + 1 -- second instruction reads R1 before it is written.

understand.visual_alt: Four pairs of instruction sequences; for each pair LHS and RHS compute the same or different final register values. The task is to identify which pairs are always equivalent.

given.aim: Determine which LHS-to-RHS replacements preserve correctness irrespective of surrounding instructions.

terms.meaning: A memory location holding some value V.
terms.connects: Both R1 and R2 get value V in the LHS of cases i-iii.

terms.meaning: The original two-instruction sequence whose effect must be matched.
terms.example: R1 <- Loc; R2 <- Loc sets both registers to the memory value.
terms.connects: The reference result that the RHS must reproduce.

terms.meaning: The replacement instruction sequence that leverages data forwarding.
terms.example: R1 <- Loc; R2 <- R1 copies via register instead of re-reading memory.
terms.connects: If forwarding can supply R1's value to the next instruction, the second memory access is eliminated.

terms.meaning: The replacement must work even if other instructions read the same registers before or after.
terms.example: If a prior instruction sets R1 to 10 and Loc = 5, a dependency on the prior R1 value must not corrupt R2.
terms.connects: This is the key constraint that rules out some replacements.

given.plan: Analyse each pair by setting concrete values for Loc, R1, R2, R3 and checking whether both sequences yield identical results. Also verify that no new read-dependencies on prior instructions are introduced.

steps.title: Analyse replacement i: R1 <- Loc, R2 <- Loc  -->  R1 <- Loc, R2 <- R1
steps.formula_raw: R2 <- Loc  vs  R2 <- R1 (where R1 just received Loc)
steps.apply: In the LHS both R1 and R2 are loaded from Loc. In the RHS, R1 is loaded from Loc and then R2 is set equal to R1. Since R1 = Loc immediately after the first instruction, R2 = R1 = Loc. The final values of R1 and R2 are identical in both sequences regardless of what was in R1 before. This replacement is safe and exactly exploits data forwarding (forwarding R1's just-written value to the next instruction). No dependency on prior instructions is introduced because the only register read in the RHS (other than Loc) is R1, which was just written.
steps.note: This is the canonical data-forwarding optimization: replace a memory read with a register copy when the register already holds the same value.

steps.title: Analyse replacement ii: R1 <- Loc, R2 <- Loc  -->  R2 <- Loc, R1 <- Loc
steps.formula_raw: Order of two independent memory reads swapped
steps.apply: Both instructions read Loc and write to different registers; they are independent. Final state: R1 = Loc, R2 = Loc in both cases. HOWEVER, the order change matters for surrounding instructions. If a subsequent instruction reads R2 immediately, in the LHS it has to wait for the 2nd instruction; in the RHS R2 is ready after the 1st instruction -- this is fine structurally. But if a PRIOR instruction writes to R1 and a subsequent instruction reads the old R1 BEFORE the first instruction of LHS executes, the reordering can expose a hazard or hide one. The key issue: the replacement changes the value of R1 and R2 at different times, which can affect surrounding pipeline stages differently. The sequences are NOT always equivalent irrespective of surrounding instructions.
steps.note: Swapping instruction order is not always safe because it changes the timing of register availability for adjacent instructions.

steps.title: Analyse replacement iii: R1 <- Loc, R2 <- Loc  -->  R2 <- Loc, R1 <- R2
steps.formula_raw: R1 <- R2 where R2 was just loaded from Loc
steps.apply: Final values: R1 = Loc, R2 = Loc in both. BUT there is an additional issue: the RHS reads R2 in the second instruction; this R2 is the one written by the first instruction of RHS. However, it ALSO matters whether a prior instruction has written R2. If a prior instruction writes R2 after the 1st RHS instruction has read Loc into R2, that prior write could be overwritten. More critically: if a later instruction expects to see the original R2 before any modification, the RHS changes R2 first, which can expose dependencies. This is NOT always equivalent irrespective of surrounding instructions.
steps.note: When a new register (R2) is introduced as an intermediate, dependencies on its prior value can be disturbed.

steps.title: Analyse replacement iv: R1 <- R2+R3, R2 <- R1+R3  -->  R1 <- R2+R3, R2 <- R2+R3+R3
steps.formula_raw:
LHS: R2_new = R1_new + R3 = (R2_old + R3) + R3 = R2_old + 2*R3
RHS: R2 <- R2 + R3 + R3 = R2_old + 2*R3
steps.apply: In LHS: R1 = R2+R3 (using old R2), then R2 = R1+R3 = (R2+R3)+R3 = R2+2*R3. In RHS: R1 = R2+R3 (same), R2 = R2+R3+R3 = R2+2*R3. Both give identical R1 and R2. The RHS eliminates the RAW dependency on R1 (the second instruction now reads R2 and R3 instead of R1). Because neither instruction in the RHS depends on what a prior instruction wrote to R1 (R1 is written first, not read), and the computation is algebraically identical, this replacement is always safe irrespective of surrounding instructions.
steps.note: This is a classic data-forwarding transformation: substitute the expression for R1 into the second instruction to break the RAW hazard.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.apply: Replacements i and iv are always correct irrespective of surrounding instructions. Replacements ii and iii can introduce hazards with surrounding instructions and are not universally safe.

questions.formula_note: Data forwarding: replace a memory/register read with a forwarded register value or algebraically substitute an expression to eliminate a RAW hazard.

questions.question:
A single-bus CPU consists of four general purpose registers R0, R1, R2, R3, ALU, IR (Instruction Register), PC, MAR, MDR, and SP (Stack Pointer). The instruction 'push r' where r = A or B has the specification:

M[SP] <- r
SP <- SP - 1

How many CPU clock cycles are required to execute the 'push r' instruction?
questions.to_find: Minimum number of CPU clock cycles to execute the 'push r' instruction on a single-bus CPU

understand.plain: A single-bus CPU means only one data transfer can happen per clock cycle on the internal bus. To execute 'push r', the CPU must store register r onto the stack (memory at address SP) and then decrement SP. Each bus transfer or ALU operation takes one clock cycle. We need to count all the micro-operations required.

keywords.explain: The CPU has one internal bus; only one register can drive the bus and one register can receive from it per clock cycle.
keywords.example: To copy R1 to MAR: R1 drives the bus, MAR latches the value -- one cycle.

keywords.explain: An elementary operation (one bus transfer or one ALU operation) that takes exactly one clock cycle.
keywords.example: MAR <- SP is one micro-operation taking one clock cycle.

keywords.explain: A register holding the address of the top of the stack; decremented after a push.
keywords.example: If SP = 100, after push SP becomes 99 and M[100] holds the pushed value.

keywords.explain: Memory Address Register holds the address for memory access; Memory Data Register holds the data to/from memory.
keywords.example: To write to memory: MAR <- address, MDR <- data, then issue memory write (takes 2 clock cycles for memory operation).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 520 340" width="520" height="340"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="0" y="0" width="520" height="340" fill="var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)" rx="10"/><text x="260" y="30" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">Single-Bus CPU: push r micro-steps</text><rect x="30" y="50" width="80" height="36" class="hi" rx="5"/><text x="70" y="73" class="tag ink" text-anchor="middle">SP</text><rect x="150" y="50" width="80" height="36" class="bg" rx="5"/><text x="190" y="73" class="tag ink" text-anchor="middle">MAR</text><rect x="270" y="50" width="80" height="36" class="bg" rx="5"/><text x="310" y="73" class="tag ink" text-anchor="middle">MDR</text><rect x="390" y="50" width="80" height="36" class="bg" rx="5"/><text x="430" y="73" class="tag ink" text-anchor="middle">r (A/B)</text><line x1="30" y1="260" x2="490" y2="260" stroke="var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)" stroke-width="4"/><text x="260" y="280" class="sub mut" text-anchor="middle">Internal Bus</text><rect x="10" y="100" width="500" height="150" fill="none" stroke="var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="4,3" rx="6"/><text x="20" y="120" class="sub ink">Cycle 1: MAR <- SP</text><text x="20" y="140" class="sub ink">Cycle 2: MDR <- r (and issue memory write, 1st cycle)</text><text x="20" y="160" class="sub ink">Cycle 3: Memory write completes (2nd cycle of mem op)</text><text x="20" y="180" class="sub ink">Cycle 4: SP <- SP - 1  (SP through ALU, decrement)</text><text x="20" y="200" class="sub ink">Cycle 5: SP latch updated</text><text x="20" y="225" class="tag ink">Total = 5 clock cycles</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing the 5 micro-operations for 'push r' on a single-bus CPU: load MAR from SP, load MDR from r, perform 2-cycle memory write, decrement SP.

given.aim: Count the minimum clock cycles to execute 'push r' (M[SP] <- r; SP <- SP - 1) on a single-bus CPU.

terms.meaning: Store register r to memory address SP, then decrement SP.
terms.connects: Defines the two semantic actions that must be micro-programmed.

terms.meaning: Each memory read or write takes 2 clock cycles on this CPU.
terms.example: Writing MDR to M[MAR] takes cycles 2 and 3.
terms.connects: Accounts for 2 of the 5 total cycles.

terms.meaning: SP <- SP - 1, done through the ALU; takes 1 clock cycle.
terms.example: SP is put on bus, ALU subtracts 1, result written back to SP.

terms.meaning: MAR <- SP and MDR <- r each take 1 clock cycle (one bus transfer each).
terms.example: Cycle 1: bus carries SP value to MAR.
terms.connects: Accounts for cycles 1 and 2 (start of memory write).

given.plan: List the sequence of micro-operations: (1) MAR <- SP, (2) MDR <- r + start memory write, (3) memory write completes, (4)-(5) SP <- SP - 1 via ALU. Count = 5.

steps.title: Micro-operation 1: Load memory address
steps.apply: The stack pointer SP is placed on the internal bus and latched into MAR. This prepares the address for the memory write. Cost: 1 clock cycle.
steps.note: On a single bus, only this transfer can happen during this cycle.

steps.title: Micro-operation 2: Load data to be written
steps.apply: Register r (A or B) is placed on the bus and latched into MDR. The memory write operation is also initiated in this cycle. Cost: 1 clock cycle.
steps.note: MDR and MAR now both hold the correct values for the memory write.

steps.title: Micro-operation 3: Complete memory write
steps.apply: The memory write completes. Memory operations take 2 cycles total; cycle 2 started it, cycle 3 completes it. Cost: 1 clock cycle.
steps.note: After this cycle, M[SP] = r, fulfilling the first part of push.

steps.title: Micro-operations 4-5: Decrement SP
steps.apply: SP is placed on the bus, passes through the ALU for subtraction of 1 (cycle 4), and the result is written back to SP (cycle 5). On some designs this can be done in 1 cycle if SP has a local decrement circuit; on a standard single-bus design it requires 2 cycles (ALU operation + write-back). Total for this part: 2 clock cycles.
steps.note: If SP can be decremented locally (as stated in GATE 2001 variant), only 1 cycle is needed here, giving 4 total. For GATE 1990 standard single-bus with no local decrement: 2 cycles here, 5 total.

steps.title: Total count
steps.apply: Cycle 1: MAR <- SP. Cycle 2: MDR <- r (start mem write). Cycle 3: mem write done. Cycle 4: SP on bus, ALU decrement. Cycle 5: SP <- ALU result. Total = 5 clock cycles.
steps.note: This is the standard answer for the GATE 1990 single-bus push instruction.

questions.formula_note: Single-bus micro-programmed CPU: each bus transfer is one clock cycle; memory operations are 2 cycles; RTL sequence for push r requires 5 micro-operations.

questions.question:
Consider the following data path of a simple non-pipelined CPU. The registers A, B, the ALU, and the MUX are 8-bit wide. PC and SP are 16-bit registers. The MUX is of size 8x1 and the memory is of size 2^16 x 8. Each memory operation takes 2 clock cycles and uses MAR (Memory Address Register) and MDR (Memory Data Register). SP can be decremented locally.

The CPU instruction 'push r' where r = A or B has the specification:
M[SP] <- r
SP <- SP - 1

How many CPU clock cycles are required to execute the 'push r' instruction?

A. 2
B. 3
C. 4
D. 5
questions.to_find: Number of CPU clock cycles to execute 'push r' when SP can be decremented locally

understand.plain: This is the same push instruction as in the previous question, but the CPU here has a special feature: SP can be decremented locally without going through the ALU on the main bus. This saves one clock cycle. Memory write still takes 2 cycles. We count each micro-operation as one clock cycle.

keywords.explain: SP has its own dedicated decrement circuit, so SP <- SP - 1 can happen in a single clock cycle without using the ALU or main bus.
keywords.example: Rather than SP -> bus -> ALU -> bus -> SP (2 cycles), the local circuit does it in 1 cycle.

keywords.explain: Instructions execute one at a time, fully completing before the next starts.
keywords.example: All micro-operations for 'push r' complete before any other instruction begins.

keywords.explain: Any memory read or write takes exactly 2 clock cycles.
keywords.example: Writing MDR to M[MAR]: cycle N starts the write, cycle N+1 completes it.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 500 300" width="500" height="300"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="0" y="0" width="500" height="300" fill="var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)" rx="10"/><text x="250" y="28" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">push r: 4-cycle execution (SP local decrement)</text><rect x="20" y="50" width="90" height="34" class="hi" rx="4"/><text x="65" y="72" class="tag ink" text-anchor="middle">Cycle 1</text><text x="120" y="72" class="sub ink">MAR <- SP  (load address)</text><rect x="20" y="95" width="90" height="34" class="bg" rx="4"/><text x="65" y="117" class="tag ink" text-anchor="middle">Cycle 2</text><text x="120" y="117" class="sub ink">MDR <- r  (load data + start mem write)</text><rect x="20" y="140" width="90" height="34" class="bg" rx="4"/><text x="65" y="162" class="tag ink" text-anchor="middle">Cycle 3</text><text x="120" y="162" class="sub ink">Memory write completes  M[MAR] <- MDR</text><rect x="20" y="185" width="90" height="34" class="hi" rx="4"/><text x="65" y="207" class="tag ink" text-anchor="middle">Cycle 4</text><text x="120" y="207" class="sub ink">SP <- SP - 1  (local decrement, 1 cycle)</text><rect x="20" y="240" width="460" height="40" fill="var(--ok,#1D9E75)" rx="6" opacity="0.18"/><text x="250" y="266" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">Total = 4 clock cycles</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Timeline of 4 clock cycles for push r: cycle 1 loads MAR from SP, cycle 2 loads MDR from r and starts memory write, cycle 3 completes the memory write, cycle 4 decrements SP locally.

given.aim: Count clock cycles for 'push r' (M[SP] <- r; SP <- SP - 1) when SP has local decrement capability.

terms.meaning: Load the stack pointer address into MAR for the upcoming memory write.
terms.connects: 1st cycle; sets up the memory write address.

terms.meaning: Load register r (A or B) into MDR; simultaneously initiate the memory write.
Second cycle of the memory operation; M[MAR] is updated with MDR's value.
terms.connects: 3rd cycle; finishes writing r to the stack.

terms.meaning: SP is decremented by its own local circuit in a single clock cycle, without the ALU.
terms.connects: 4th cycle; saves 1 cycle compared to the non-local version.

given.plan: Enumerate the 4 micro-operations: (1) MAR <- SP, (2) MDR <- r + start mem-write, (3) mem-write complete, (4) SP <- SP - 1 locally. Total = 4.

steps.title: Cycle 1: Set up memory address
steps.apply: The current value of SP is placed on the data bus and loaded into MAR. This establishes the target address for the memory write. Cost: 1 cycle.
steps.note: PC and SP are 16-bit; MAR must also be 16-bit to hold the full address.

steps.title: Cycle 2: Set up data and start memory write
steps.apply: Register r (A or B, 8-bit) is placed on the bus and loaded into MDR. The memory write operation begins in this cycle. Cost: 1 cycle.
steps.note: MDR and r are both 8-bit; memory is 8-bit wide (2^16 x 8), so one byte is written.

steps.title: Cycle 3: Complete memory write
steps.apply: The memory write operation completes (2nd of 2 memory cycles). M[SP_old] now holds the value of r. Cost: 1 cycle.
steps.note: First part of the push semantics (M[SP] <- r) is now done.

steps.title: Cycle 4: Decrement SP locally
steps.apply: Because SP can be decremented locally (without using the ALU and internal bus), this operation completes in exactly 1 clock cycle. SP now points to the new top of stack. Cost: 1 cycle.
steps.note: This is the key difference from a standard single-bus CPU where SP decrement would need 2 cycles (via ALU). Local decrement saves 1 cycle, giving 4 total instead of 5.

steps.title: Total clock cycles
steps.apply: Cycle 1: MAR <- SP. Cycle 2: MDR <- r (start mem write). Cycle 3: Mem write done. Cycle 4: SP <- SP - 1 (local). Total = 4 clock cycles.

questions.formula_note: RTL micro-programming for push on a single-bus CPU: 2 cycles for memory write + 1 cycle for MAR setup + 1 cycle for MDR load + 1 cycle for local SP decrement = 4 cycles total.
